title: Polymer clay braided bangle
subtitle: Three ropes of contrasting colour, braided around a jar and oven-cured into a wearable bangle
excerpt: Roll three ropes of contrasting polymer clay, braid them together, wrap the braid around a jar mandrel, join the ends, and cure in a domestic oven at 120 degrees C. No kiln, no wheel, no special equipment needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: Warming and kneading polymer clay until it is soft, pliable, and free of air pockets before use
Baking polymer clay in a domestic oven at the manufacturer's recommended temperature to harden it permanently through a chemical change in the PVC binders

content.text: A braided bangle is a satisfying polymer clay project: it asks for even rope thickness, a neat braid, and a clean join, with no equipment beyond a work surface and a domestic oven. The three-rope braid gives strong colour contrast and a finished piece that looks jewellery-quality.
Conditioning
the clay well is the key step: under-conditioned clay will crack when bent around the mandrel.
Curing
at 120 degrees C for 30 minutes gives a durable, flexible bangle.

materials.name: Polymer clay, colour 3 (e.g. black or gold)

tools.name: Pottery rolling pin or smooth dowel
Round jar or bottle as mandrel (matching wrist size)
Baking paper or ceramic tile for curing

content.text: Preparation

Measure your wrist circumference and choose a jar or bottle slightly smaller than your wrist to allow for the thickness of the braid. A jar that is 5 to 10 mm smaller in diameter than your wrist is correct. Wrap the jar loosely in baking paper so the cured bangle releases easily. Preheat the oven to 120 degrees C and place an oven thermometer inside: domestic ovens vary widely and overheating scorches polymer clay.
Conditioning the clay

Take 20 g of each colour and condition each block separately. Press, fold, and roll the clay repeatedly until it feels soft, smooth, and warm with no stiff patches. Cold, under-conditioned clay will crack when bent. Two to three minutes of active kneading per block is the minimum.
Test each block by folding it sharply. Conditioned clay folds without cracking or flaking. If cracks appear at the fold, continue conditioning.
Rolling the ropes

Roll each conditioned block into a rope approximately 6 mm in diameter. Work on a smooth, non-porous surface. Apply even pressure along the full length, not just at one end. All three ropes must be the same diameter or the braid will be uneven.
Measure the circumference of your chosen mandrel and add 25 mm for the join overlap. Cut each rope to this length with the fettling knife. A clean perpendicular cut at each end makes joining easier.
Pinch the three ropes together at one end and press the joined ends onto the baking paper on the work surface to anchor them while you braid.
Braiding

Braid the three ropes using a standard three-strand braid: right over centre, then left over centre, alternating. Keep the tension even throughout. A loose braid will look floppy after curing; an overly tight braid will distort the ropes into oval cross-sections.
Stop braiding approximately 15 mm before the cut ends. Do not braid all the way to the tips, as you need unbraided length for joining around the mandrel.
Forming on the mandrel

Lift the braided rope and carefully wrap it around the jar mandrel. The braid should sit flat against the jar with no gaps and no stretching. Position the unbraided end tails so they meet the starting tails at the same point on the jar.

Match each colour tail from the start end to its corresponding tail from the finish end. Pinch matching pairs together and blend the join by rolling the joint between your fingertips. Work through all three pairs in sequence. The finished join should feel as smooth as the rest of the rope diameter.

Continue the braid pattern across the join if any unbraided section remains, pressing the finished join flat against the mandrel. Use the optional needle tool to smooth any surface marks or fingerprints at the join area.
Check that the bangle sits level on the mandrel without tilting. Gently press any high point down and roll the whole form lightly between your palms (keeping the jar inside) to even the profile.
Curing

Place the jar with the bangle still on it onto a baking paper-lined tray and place in the preheated oven. Cure at 120 degrees C for 30 minutes. Do not exceed 130 degrees C or the clay may darken and become brittle. After curing, leave the bangle on the mandrel until it is fully cool to the touch, approximately 20 minutes. The bangle will flex very slightly during cooling as the clay contracts; do not disturb it during this phase.
Finishing

Once cool, slide the bangle off the jar. Sand any rough spots with 400-grit wet-dry paper used wet, then move to 800-grit for a smooth surface. A final pass with 1200-grit followed by buffing on a soft cloth brings up a low sheen. If a higher gloss is wanted, apply a thin coat of polymer clay-compatible varnish to the exterior only.
Variations
Five-rope flat braid: add two extra ropes for a wider, flatter bangle profile that sits differently on the wrist.
Metallic accent: use a gold or silver-toned polymer clay for one rope to pick up light across the braid.
Cane slice inserts: roll short lengths of a patterned cane into the braided rope before wrapping to add repeating pattern detail.

rows.problem: Rope cracked when bent around the mandrel
rows.cause: Clay was under-conditioned or had cooled down during rolling
rows.fix: Re-warm and condition the rope by pressing and folding it again. Work faster once conditioned so the clay does not cool between rolling and bending.

rows.problem: Join is visible as a colour-break in the braid
rows.cause: The rope ends were not blended thoroughly enough at the join
rows.fix: Roll the join firmly between finger and thumb in small circular motions until the seam disappears into the surface. Check colour continuity before curing.

rows.problem: Bangle stuck to the jar mandrel after curing
rows.cause: The mandrel was not wrapped in baking paper, or the clay shrank tightly around the jar
rows.fix: Allow the jar to cool completely, then gently twist the bangle rather than pulling it straight off. A thin smear of petroleum jelly on the paper before wrapping prevents adhesion.

rows.problem: Bangle came out darker or discoloured after curing
rows.cause: Oven temperature was too high or cure time too long
rows.fix: Use an oven thermometer and check at the 25-minute mark. Discoloured polymer cannot be reversed, but lighter colours are more forgiving; test with a scrap piece before curing the final piece.
